text: Endurance athletes typically demonstrate better cardiac autonomic function than non-athletes, with lower resting heart rates and greater heart rate variability.
Key factors affecting resting HRV include exercise intensity, duration, modality, overall training load, and lifestyle factors such as sleep and stress.
HRV analysis requires attention to stationarity of data, body position, timing and duration of the recording window, and average heart rate to produce valid measurements.
HRV-guided training, where daily training intensity is adjusted based on HRV readings, is a practical application for optimizing endurance training load.
HRV monitoring can be used to identify and monitor maladaptive states such as overtraining syndrome in endurance athletes.
Sex and age differences are important confounding variables that must be accounted for when interpreting HRV data.
HRV can be applied beyond resting measurements, including during exercise, post-exercise recovery monitoring, and for physiological forecasting and performance prediction.
